name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
هَلْ أَتَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ حِينٌ مِّنَ ٱلدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُن شَيْـًٔا مَّذْكُورًا (١)
```

```english
Has there been a period of time for man when he was not anything worthy of mention? (1)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ أَمْشَاجٍ نَّبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (٢)
```

```english
Indeed We created man from the drop of a mixed fluid so that We may put him to test, so We endowed with hearing and sight. (2)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا هَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلسَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شَاكِرًا وَإِمَّا كَفُورًا (٣)
```

```english
Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful. (3)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَلَـٰسِلَا۟ وَأَغْلَـٰلًا وَسَعِيرًا (٤)
```

```english
Indeed for the faithless We have prepared chains, iron collars, and a blaze. (4)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِن كَأْسٍ كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا كَافُورًا (٥)
```

```english
Indeed the pious will drink from a cup seasoned with Kafur, (5)
```

```arabic
عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا عِبَادُ ٱللَّهِ يُفَجِّرُونَهَا تَفْجِيرًا (٦)
```

```english
a spring where Allah’s servants will drink, making it gush forth as they please. (6)
```

```arabic
يُوفُونَ بِٱلنَّذْرِ وَيَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا كَانَ شَرُّهُۥ مُسْتَطِيرًا (٧)
```

```english
They fulfill their vows and fear a day whose ill will be widespread. (7)
```

```arabic
وَيُطْعِمُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ مِسْكِينًا وَيَتِيمًا وَأَسِيرًا (٨)
```

```english
For the love of Him, they feed the needy, the orphan and the prisoner, (8)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ لَا نُرِيدُ مِنكُمْ جَزَآءً وَلَا شُكُورًا (٩)
```

```english
[saying,] ‘We feed you only for the sake of Allah. We desire no reward from you, nor thanks. (9)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا نَخَافُ مِن رَّبِّنَا يَوْمًا عَبُوسًا قَمْطَرِيرًا (١٠)
```

```english
Indeed we fear a frowning and fateful day from our Lord.’ (10)
```

```arabic
فَوَقَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ شَرَّ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمِ وَلَقَّىٰهُمْ نَضْرَةً وَسُرُورًا (١١)
```

```english
So Allah saved them from that day’s ills and graced them with freshness [on this faces] and joy [in their hearts]. (11)
```

```arabic
وَجَزَىٰهُم بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ جَنَّةً وَحَرِيرًا (١٢)
```

```english
He rewarded them for their patience with a garden and [garments of] silk, (12)
```

```arabic
مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ ۖ لَا يَرَوْنَ فِيهَا شَمْسًا وَلَا زَمْهَرِيرًا (١٣)
```

```english
reclining therein on couches, without facing any [scorching] sun, or [biting] cold. (13)
```

```arabic
وَدَانِيَةً عَلَيْهِمْ ظِلَـٰلُهَا وَذُلِّلَتْ قُطُوفُهَا تَذْلِيلًا (١٤)
```

```english
Its shades will be close over them and its clusters [of fruits] will be hanging low. (14)
```

```arabic
وَيُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِـَٔانِيَةٍ مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ كَانَتْ قَوَارِيرَا۠ (١٥)
```

```english
They will be served around with vessels of silver and goblets of crystal (15)
```

```arabic
قَوَارِيرَا۟ مِن فِضَّةٍ قَدَّرُوهَا تَقْدِيرًا (١٦)
```

```english
—crystal of silver— [from] which they will dispense in a precise measure. (16)
```

```arabic
وَيُسْقَوْنَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا زَنجَبِيلًا (١٧)
```

```english
They will be served therein with a cup of a drink seasoned with Zanjabeel, (17)
```

```arabic
عَيْنًا فِيهَا تُسَمَّىٰ سَلْسَبِيلًا (١٨)
```

```english
from a spring in it named Salsabeel. (18)
```

```arabic
۞ وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدَٰنٌ مُّخَلَّدُونَ إِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ حَسِبْتَهُمْ لُؤْلُؤًا مَّنثُورًا (١٩)
```

```english
They will be waited upon by immortal youths, whom, were you to see them, you will suppose them to be scattered pearls. (19)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيمًا وَمُلْكًا كَبِيرًا (٢٠)
```

```english
As you look on, you will see there bliss and a great kingdom. (20)
```

```arabic
عَـٰلِيَهُمْ ثِيَابُ سُندُسٍ خُضْرٌ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٌ ۖ وَحُلُّوٓا۟ أَسَاوِرَ مِن فِضَّةٍ وَسَقَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَرَابًا طَهُورًا (٢١)
```

```english
Upon them will be cloaks of green silk and brocade and they will be adorned with bracelets of silver. Their Lord will give them to drink a pure drink. (21)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَآءً وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُم مَّشْكُورًا (٢٢)
```

```english
[They will be told]: ‘This is your reward, and your efforts have been well-appreciated.’ (22)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the creation of man after he was nothing to be mentioned, then His guiding him to the path, whether he be grateful or ungrateful. It states that Allah has prepared various types of punishment for the disbelievers and diverse forms of bliss for the righteous (الأبرار) — and He has detailed the description of their bliss in eighteen verses, which is evidence that this is the primary objective of the exposition.

Then, addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it mentions that the Quran is a revelation from Him, the Exalted, unto him and a reminder; therefore, let him be patient for the judgment of his Lord, and let him not follow the people in their desires. Let him remember the Name of his Lord morning and evening, prostrate to Him during the night, and glorify Him for a long part of the night.

The Surah is entirely Medinan (مدنية), or at least its opening — which consists of twenty-two verses from its beginning — is Medinan, while its conclusion — which consists of nine verses from its end — is Meccan (مكية). The narrations of the People of the House (أهل البيت) (عليه‌السلام) are unanimous regarding its being Medinan, and the narrations of the Sunnis are extensively reported (مستفيضة) to that effect.

It has also been said that it is entirely Meccan, and the detailed discussion regarding this will reach you in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah the Exalted willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُورا** — *Has there been a period of time for man when he was not anything worthy of mention?* (76:1). The interrogation is for affirmation (التقرير), thus it signifies the established reality and realization of the sentence's meaning; i.e., "Indeed, there has come upon man..." etc. Perhaps this is what was intended by those among the early exegetes who said that "هل" in the verse is in the sense of "قد" (indeed), not that this is one of the linguistic meanings of "هل," as some have mentioned.

What is meant by "man" (الإنسان) is the genus. As for the claim of some that it refers to Adam (عليه‌السلام), it is not compatible with His saying in the following verse: **إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ** — *Indeed We created man from a drop of fluid* (76:2).

A "period" (الحين) is a limited segment of time, whether short or long, while "time" (الدهر) is extended time without being delimited by a beginning or an end.

His saying: **شَيْئاً مَذْكُوراً** — *anything worthy of mention* means a thing mentioned by its name among mentioned things. For example, the earth, the sky, the land, the sea, and other things were mentioned, but man was not mentioned because he did not yet exist. When he came into existence, it was said: "Man." Thus, his being "mentioned" is an allusion (كناية) to his being actually existent. Therefore, the negation in His saying: **لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُوراً** — *he was not anything worthy of mention* is directed toward his being a "mentioned thing," not toward the essence of his being "a thing." For he was a thing, but he was not a mentioned thing. This is supported by His saying: **إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ** — *Indeed We created man from a drop of fluid* etc., for he existed in his matter but had not yet been formed into an actual human being. The verse and those following it occur in the context of argumentation (الاحتجاج), explaining through them that man is a temporal phenomenon (حادث) who requires in his existence a Maker to make him and a Creator to create him. His Lord created him and equipped him through Divine governance (التدبير الربوبي) with the instruments of perception (أدوات الشعور), namely hearing and sight, by which he is guided to the path of truth (السبيل الحق) that he must traverse throughout his life. If he disbelieves, his destiny is a painful punishment, and if he is grateful, it is an abiding bliss.

The meaning is: Has there come — indeed there has come — upon man a limited segment of this extended, unlimited time, while the state was such that he was not actually existent, mentioned among the ranks of mentioned things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ أَمْشاجٍ نَبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْناهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً** — *Indeed We created man from a drop of fluid, a mixture, to test him. So We made him endowed with hearing and sight* (76:2). The "drop of fluid" (النطفة) originally means a small amount of water; its usage became predominant for the male fluid of animals from which their like is formed. "Mixture" (أمشاج) is the plural of *mashīj* (مشيج) or *mashaj* (المشج), meaning mixed or blended. The fluid is described by it in consideration of its different parts or the mixing of the male and female fluids.

"Testing" (الابتلاء) is the moving of a thing from state to state and from stage to stage, like the testing of gold in a crucible. His, the Exalted, testing of man in his creation from the drop of fluid is what He mentioned in various places of His speech: that He creates the drop of fluid, then makes it a clinging mass (علقة), then the clinging mass a fleshy lump (مضغة), until the end of the stages that succeed one another until He brings him forth as another creation.

It has been said: What is meant by "testing him" is his trial through religious obligation (التكليف). This is refuted by the fact that His saying: **فَجَعَلْناهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً** — *So We made him endowed with hearing and sight* is branched (تفريع) upon the testing. If the meaning were religious obligation, it would have been necessary to branch it upon making him hearing and seeing, not the reverse. The response that there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech — and the intended meaning is: "We created him from a mixed drop of fluid and made him hearing and seeing so that We might test him" — is not to be heeded.

Regarding His saying: **فَجَعَلْناهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً** — *So We made him endowed with hearing and sight*, the context of the verses, especially His saying: **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ** — *Indeed We have guided him to the way* etc., indicates that the mention of making him hearing and seeing is to use it as a means in the Divine governance toward its goal. This goal is for him to see the signs (آيات) of Allah indicating the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and to hear the word of truth that comes to him from his Lord through the sending of messengers and the revelation of books. Thus, sight and hearing call him to traverse the path of truth and to proceed in the course of life with faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). If he adheres to the path to which he was guided, it leads him to eternal bliss; otherwise, to an everlasting punishment.

The mention of "man" (الإنسان) in the verse is an instance of placing a manifest noun in the place of a pronoun, and the subtle point (النكتة) in this is to affirm that He, the Exalted, is his Creator and the Governor of his affairs.

The meaning is: Indeed, We created man from a drop of fluid that consists of mixed and blended parts, while the state is such that We move him from state to state and stage to stage; so We made him hearing and seeing so that he may hear what comes to him of the Divine call and see the Divine signs indicating His Oneness, the Prophethood, and the Return.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** — *Indeed We have guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful* (76:3). Guidance (الهداية) here is in the sense of showing the way (إراءة الطريق), not reaching the goal. What is meant by "the way" (السبيل) is the way in the true sense of the word, which is that which leads to the desired goal, namely the path of truth.

Gratitude (الشكر) is the use of a blessing while manifesting that it is from its Bestower. It has been previously mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: **وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ** — *and Allah will reward the grateful* (3:144), that the reality of a servant being grateful to Allah is his being sincere (مخلصا) to his Lord. Ingratitude (الكفران) is the use of the blessing while concealing that it is from the Bestower.

His saying: **إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** — *whether he be grateful or ungrateful* are two circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) for the pronoun in "guided him" (هَدَيْناهُ), not for "the way" (السَّبِيلَ) as some have said. "Either/or" (إِمَّا) signifies division and categorization; i.e., We guided him to the way while he is divided into the grateful and the ungrateful; meaning he is guided regardless of whether he is this or that.

The expression **إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** is evidence, firstly: that what is meant by "the way" is the tradition (السنة) and the method that man must traverse in his worldly life to reach his happiness in this world and the Hereafter, and which drives him to the honor of proximity and nearness to his Lord; its sum total is the true religion (الدين الحق), which with Allah is Islam (الإسلام).

Through this, it becomes clear that the interpretation by some of "the way" as the way of exiting the womb is not sound.

Secondly: that the way to which he is guided is a path of choice (اختياري), and that gratitude and ingratitude, which result from the aforementioned guidance, occur within the domain of man's free will (الاختيار) — he may clothe himself with whichever he wishes without compulsion or coercion, as He, the Exalted, said: **ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ** — *then He made the way easy for him* (80:20). What appears at the end of the Surah in His saying: **فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً وَما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ** — *so let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord. But you do not wish unless it is wished by Allah* (76:29-30) only signifies the attachment of His, the Exalted, will to the will of the servant, not to the act of the servant to which the servant's will is attached, such that it would signify the negation of the efficacy of the servant's will regarding his act. This meaning has been pointed out repeatedly in this book.

The guidance which is a type of notification and informing from Him, the Exalted, to man is a primordial guidance (هداية فطرية) — it is an awakening due to the nature of his creation and that with which his existence is equipped, by inspiration from Allah, the Glorified, toward the truth of belief and the righteousness of action. He, the Exalted, said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها** — *by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its Godwariness* (91:7-8). Broader in its implication is His saying: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — *So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature (الفطرة) — the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of Allah which He has fashioned in mankind. There is no changing Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion* (30:30).

And there is a verbal guidance (هداية قولية) by way of the call (الدعوة), through the raising of prophets, the sending of messengers, the revelation of books, and the legislation of Divine laws. Divine governance has never ceased to support human life with the religious call carried out by His prophets and messengers, thereby reinforcing the call of the primordial nature (الفطرة), as He said: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — *We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him* — until He said — **رُسُلاً مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ** — *apostles, as bearers of good news and as warners, so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles* (163-165).

Among the differences between the two guidances is that the primordial guidance is universal and reaching (بالغة); no human is excluded from it because it is a necessary concomitant of human creation, and it is equal among individuals. However, its effect may weaken or become nullified due to factors and causes that distract man and turn him away from attending to what his intellect calls him to and what his primordial nature guides him toward, or due to wicked traits and states that prevent him from answering the call of the primordial nature, such as stubbornness, obstinacy, and the like. He, the Exalted, said: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً فَمَنْ يَهْدِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللهِ** — *Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah?* (45:23). The guidance negated in the verse is in the sense of reaching the goal, not showing the way, as evidenced by His saying: "and whom Allah has led astray knowingly."

As for the verbal guidance, which is contained in the religious call, its nature is to reach the society and be presented to the intellects of the group; thus, whoever prefers truth over falsehood returns to it. As for its reaching every single individual among them, the causes and means used to explain such objectives might not facilitate that, given the differences in circumstances, times, and environments. How can a human call every single human to what he wants by himself or through intermediaries of his kind? That is extremely difficult.

To the first meaning, He, the Exalted, pointed by His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ** — *and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it* (35:24). And to the second by His saying: **لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أُنْذِرَ آباؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غافِلُونَ** — *so that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, so they are oblivious* (36:6).

So, whoever the call has reached and the truth has been disclosed to him, the argument (الحجة) has been completed against him. And whoever the call has not reached in a way that the truth is disclosed to him, the Divine grace (الفضل الإلهي) reaches him thereafter as one who is "deemed weak" (مستضعفا); his affair is with Allah: if He wishes, He forgives him, and if He wishes, He punishes him. He, the Exalted, said: **إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَالنِّساءِ وَالْوِلْدانِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلاً** — *except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither the resources nor are guided to any way* (4:98).

Furthermore, among the evidence that the Divine call — which is the guidance to the way — is a truth that man must follow, is the primordial nature of man and his creation equipped with that which guides to it of belief and action. Also, the actual occurrence of the call through prophethood and messengership. For the happiness of every existent and its perfection (كمال) lie in the effects and actions that suit its essence and harmonize with it through the powers and instruments with which it is equipped. Thus, the happiness of man and his perfection lie in following the Divine religion, which is the tradition of primordial life (سنة الحياة الفطرية); the intellect has judged it so, and the prophets and messengers (عليه‌السلام) have brought it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَلاسِلَ وَأَغْلالاً وَسَعِيراً** — *Indeed We have prepared for the faithless chains, iron collars, and a blazing fire* (76:4). "Preparation" (الاعتاد) is to make ready. "Chains" (سلاسل) is the plural of *silsilah*, which is the shackle by which the criminal is led. "Iron collars" (أغلال) is the plural of *ghull*; it is said to be the shackle that joins the hands to the neck. Al-Raghib said: "The *ghull* is specific to that which binds, such that the limbs are placed in its middle." End quote. "Blazing fire" (السعير) is the ignited fire. The meaning is clear.

The verse points to the consequence for the "ungrateful" (الكفور) man mentioned in His saying: **إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً**. The exposition of his consequence was placed before the exposition of the reward for the grateful man for the sake of brevity in its description.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِنْ كَأْسٍ كانَ مِزاجُها كافُوراً** — *Indeed the pious shall drink from a cup whose mixture is camphor* (76:5). A "cup" (الكأس) is a vessel for drink when it contains drink. A "mixture" (المزاج) is that which is mixed in, like a belt (*hizām*) for that which is belted (*hazama*). "Camphor" (الكافور) is well-known and is used as a proverb for coolness and pleasant fragrance. It has been said: It is the name of a spring in Paradise.

"The righteous" (الأبرار) is the plural of *barr*, an adjective derived from *birr*, which is "righteousness (البر) — *the act of doing good and being dutiful*." Its meaning is realized in a person doing good in his action without desiring a benefit returning to him in the form of reward or thanks. He desires the good because it is good, not because there is a benefit returning to himself, even if his soul dislikes it. Thus, he is patient (يصبر) over the bitterness of opposing his soul in what it desires, and he performs the act because it is good in itself, such as fulfilling a vow, or because there is good in it for others, such as feeding food to the deserving among the servants of Allah.

Since there is no good in an action nor any righteousness except through faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, as He, the Exalted, said: **أُولئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا فَأَحْبَطَ اللهُ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — *They have no faith, so Allah has made their works fail* (33:19), among other verses.

Therefore, the righteous are believers in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day. Since their faith is a faith of maturity and insight, they see themselves as owned servants of their Lord; to Him belong their creation and their command. They possess for themselves neither benefit nor harm. It is upon them not to desire except what their Lord desires and not to do except what He is pleased with. Thus, they prioritized His will over their own will and acted for Him; they were patient over opposing their souls in what they crave and love and the burden of obedience, and they did what they did for the sake of Allah (لوجه الله). Thus, they were sincere in their servitude (العبودية) to Allah, the Glorified, at the stage of action.

These are the attributes by which the Glorified defined the righteous, as inferred from His saying: **يَشْرَبُ بِها عِبادُ اللهِ** — *a spring wherefrom the servants of Allah shall drink* (76:6), and His saying: **إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ** — *We feed you only for the sake of Allah* (76:9), and His saying: **وَجَزاهُمْ بِما صَبَرُوا** — *and He will reward them for their patience* (76:12). These are also inferred from His saying in their description: **لَيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَلكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ** — *Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah* etc. (2:177). Some discussion on the meaning of *birr* has passed in the commentary of that verse, and some will follow in His saying: **كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ** — *No indeed! The record of the pious is in Illiyyun* (83:18).

The verse, namely His saying: **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ** etc., by what is immediately understood from its meaning in terms of its contrast to His saying: **إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ** etc., which explains the state of the disbelievers in the Hereafter, explains the state of the righteous in the Hereafter in Paradise: that they drink from a drink mixed with camphor, cool and pleasantly fragrant.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِها عِبادُ اللهِ يُفَجِّرُونَها تَفْجِيراً** — *a spring wherefrom the servants of Allah shall drink, making it gush forth as they please* (76:6). "A spring" (عَيْناً) is in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition — the estimation being "from a spring" — or due to specification (الاختصاص) — the estimation being "I specify a spring." Drinking (الشرب) — as has been said — can be transitive by itself or by the preposition *bā'*; thus "drank with it" and "drank it" are the same. Describing them as "servants of Allah" (عباد الله) is to point to their being adorned with the ornament of servitude and their fulfillment of its requirements, as indicated by the context of praise.

"Gushing forth the spring" (تفجير العين) is to cleave the earth to make it flow. Their gushing forth the spring should be understood as their will for its flowing, because the blessings of Paradise do not require for their realization and enjoyment more than the will of its inhabitants. He, the Exalted, said: **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها** — *They will have therein whatever they wish* (50:35).

The two verses — as previously pointed out — describe the enjoyment of the righteous with the drink of Paradise in the Hereafter, and this is how the two verses have been interpreted.

It is not improbable that the two verses are structured according to the path of the "embodiment of deeds" (تجسم الأعمال), describing the reality of their righteous deeds of fulfilling vows and feeding food for the sake of Allah; and that their aforementioned deeds, according to their inner reality (باطنها), are a drinking from a cup whose mixture is camphor from a spring that they never cease to make gush forth through their righteous deeds, and which will appear to them in its true reality in the Paradise of Eternity, even if it was in the world in the form of deeds. Thus, the two verses would follow the course of such sayings of His, the Exalted: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنا فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ أَغْلالاً فَهِيَ إِلَى الْأَدْقانِ فَهُمْ مُقْمَحُونَ** — *Indeed We have put iron collars around their necks, which reach up to their chins, so their heads are forced up* (36:8).

This is supported by the apparent meaning of His saying: **يَشْرَبُونَ** (they drink) and **يَشْرَبُ بِها** (they drink from it), and He did not say: "they will drink" (سيشربون). Also, the occurrence of His saying: "they drink," "they fulfill," "they fear," and "they feed" consecutively in a single context. Furthermore, the mention of "gushing forth" in His saying: **يُفَجِّرُونَها تَفْجِيراً**, which is apparent in extracting the spring and making it flow by resorting to causes.

Regarding the individual words of the two verses and their grammatical analysis, there are many different opinions mentioned in the lengthy works; let whoever wishes to examine them refer there.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ وَيَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً** — *They fulfill their vows and fear a day whose ill is widespread* (76:7). "Widespread" (المستطير) is the active participle from *istatāra* when something spreads and diffuses in all directions to the utmost extent; it is more emphatic than *tāra* (flew), as it is said: the fire *istatārat* and the dawn *istatāra* when they expand to their limit. What is meant by the widespread nature of the day's ill — which is the Day of Resurrection — is that its hardships, terrors, and the punishment therein reach their utmost limit.

What is meant by "fulfilling the vow" (الإيفاء بالنذر) is its well-known apparent meaning. The claim of some that it refers to what they have resolved in their hearts of performing obligatory acts, or what they have resolved of following the Lawgiver in all that He has legislated, is contrary to the apparent meaning of the word without any evidence to indicate it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى حُبِّهِ مِسْكِيناً وَيَتِيماً وَأَسِيراً** — *They give food, for the love of Him, to the needy, the orphan and the prisoner* (76:8). The pronoun in "for the love of it" (عَلى حُبِّهِ) refers to the food, as is apparent. What is meant by "love of it" is the soul's longing for it due to intense need. This meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **لَنْ تَنالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّى تُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ** — *You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear* (3:92).

It has been said: The pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified; i.e., they feed the food out of love for Allah, not out of greed for reward. This is refuted by the fact that His saying, the Exalted, narrating from them: **إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ** — *We feed you only for the sake of Allah* (76:9) makes it redundant.

Equally weak is the claim that the pronoun refers to "feeding" (الإطعام) inferred from His saying: "and they feed." The reason for its weakness is that if the literal meaning of "love of feeding" is intended, there is no merit in the love of feeding in itself such that they would be praised for it. And if it means that the feeding is done with a pleasant soul and without affectation, it is contrary to the apparent meaning. The pronoun's return to "the food" is the apparent meaning.

The meanings of "the needy" (المسكين) and "the orphan" (اليتيم) are known. What is meant by "the prisoner" (الأسير) is its apparent meaning, which is the one taken from the people of the Abode of War (دار الحرب).

The claims of some that it refers to the prisoners of Badr, or the prisoner from the people of the Qiblah in the Abode of War in the hands of the disbelievers, or the incarcerated, or the owned slave, or the wife — all of this is affectation (تكلف) without any evidence to indicate it.

What must be noted is that the context of these verses is the context of narration (الاقتصاص); they mention a group of believers whom they call "the righteous" (الأبرار), disclose some of their deeds — which is the fulfillment of a vow and the feeding of a needy person, an orphan, and a prisoner — praise them, and promise them a beautiful promise.

Thus, what they point to of the story is the "occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) — *the specific historical context or event that prompted the revelation of a Quranic verse*." Their context is not the context of assuming a hypothetical subject and mentioning its beautiful effects, then the beautiful promise for them. Furthermore, the inclusion of the prisoner among those whom these righteous ones fed is a fine witness to the verses being Medinan, for taking prisoners only occurred after the migration of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the triumph of Islam over disbelief and polytheism, not before it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ لا نُرِيدُ مِنْكُمْ جَزاءً وَلا شُكُوراً** — *[saying,] ‘We feed you only for the sake of Allah. We do not desire any reward from you, nor thanks’* (76:9). The "face" (وجه) of a thing is that by which it confronts another. His, the Exalted, "face" is His noble active attributes (صفاته الفعلية) by which He pours forth good upon His creation, such as creation, governance, and provision — in short, the general mercy (الرحمة العامة) by which everything subsists. The meaning of the action being "for the sake of Allah" (لوجه الله) according to this is that the goal in the action is to seek the outpouring of Allah's mercy and to seek His pleasure by restricting oneself to that and turning away from the desired reward from others. Therefore, they followed their saying: "We feed you only for the sake of Allah" with their saying: "We do not desire any reward from you, nor thanks."

Beyond that are His noble essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) which are the origin of His active attributes and the good that results from them in the world. The return of the action being "for the sake of Allah" according to this is performing the action out of love for Allah because He is the Absolute Beauty. Or if you wish, say: worshipping Him, the Exalted, because He is worthy of worship.

Seeking the "face of Allah" (ابتغاء وجه الله) by making it a motivating goal in actions is mentioned in various places of His, the Exalted, speech, such as His saying: **وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ** — *And content yourself with those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face* (18:28), and His saying: **وَما تُنْفِقُونَ إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ اللهِ** — *and you do not spend except to seek Allah’s face* (2:272). In this sense is His saying: **وَما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** — *Yet they were not commanded except to worship Allah, dedicating their religion exclusively to Him* (98:5), and His saying: **فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** — *so supplicate Him, dedicating your religion exclusively to Him* (40:65), and His saying: **أَلا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخالِصُ** — *Now, surely, pure religion is for Allah* (39:3).

His saying: **لا نُرِيدُ مِنْكُمْ جَزاءً وَلا شُكُوراً** — *We do not desire any reward from you, nor thanks*. "Reward" (الجزاء) is to meet an action with its equivalent, whether good for good or evil for evil; it encompasses both action and speech. However, what is meant by it in the verse, by the evidence of its contrast with "thanks," is meeting their feeding with an action, not a tongue.

"Thanks" (الشكر) and "gratitude" (الشكور) is the mention of a blessing and its manifestation by heart, tongue, or action. What is meant by it in the verse, as it is contrasted with "reward," is beautiful praise by tongue.

The verse, namely His saying: **إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ** etc., is an address from them to those they fed — the needy, the orphan, and the prisoner. It is either by the "tongue of speech" (لسان المقال), thus it is a narration of their words; or by the estimation of speech — and in any case, they intended by it to comfort their hearts so they would be safe from reminder of favor and injury. Or it is by the "tongue of state" (لسان الحال), which is praise from Allah for them because of the sincerity He knows in their hearts.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا نَخافُ مِنْ رَبِّنا يَوْماً عَبُوساً قَمْطَرِيراً** — *Indeed we fear from our Lord a day, frowning and fateful* (76:10). Describing the day — which is the Day of Resurrection — as "frowning" (عبوسا) is a metaphor (استعارة). What is meant by its frowning is its appearance to the criminals with its complete severity. "Fateful" (القمطرير) is the difficult and severe, according to what has been said.

The verse is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for their narrated saying: "We feed you only for the sake of Allah" etc. They point out by this saying that their restricting the action to seeking the face of Allah, the Exalted, out of sincerity in servitude, was due to their fear of that severe day. They did not suffice with attributing the fear to the day until they attributed it in a certain way to their Lord, saying: "We fear from our Lord a day..." etc. This is because, since they desired nothing but the face of their Lord, they fear none other than Him, just as they hope in none other than Him. They only fear the Day of Resurrection because it is from their Lord, wherein He calls His servants to account for their deeds and rewards them accordingly.

As for His saying previously: **وَيَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً** — *and fear a day whose ill is widespread*, where He attributed their fear to the day, the describer therein is Allah, the Glorified, and He had already attributed the day with its hardships to Himself previously when He said: **إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَلاسِلَ** etc.

In short, the fear they mentioned is a fear in the station of action, because the servant is called to account for his action. Thus, servitude is necessary for man and does not leave him, no matter what he attains. He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ إِلَيْنا إِيابَهُمْ ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا حِسابَهُمْ** — *Indeed to Us is their return, then indeed upon Us is their reckoning* (88:25-26).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَوَقاهُمُ اللهُ شَرَّ ذلِكَ الْيَوْمِ وَلَقَّاهُمْ نَضْرَةً وَسُرُوراً** — *So Allah saved them from the ill of that day, and He granted them freshness and joy* (76:11). "Saving" (الوقاية) is protection and prevention from harm. "Granting" (لقي بكذا) means He met them with it. "Freshness" (النضرة) is radiance and beauty of color, and "joy" (السرور) is the opposite of distress and grief.

The meaning is: So Allah protected them and prevented from them the ill of that day, and He met them with freshness and joy. Thus, they are radiant of face and joyful on that day, as He said: **وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ** — *Some faces on that day will be fresh* (75:22).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَزاهُمْ بِما صَبَرُوا جَنَّةً وَحَرِيراً** — *and He will reward them for their patience with a garden and [garments of] silk* (76:12). What is meant by "patience" (الصبر) is their patience during affliction, upon obedience, and away from disobedience. For they sought in the world the face of their Lord and prioritized His will over their own will; so they were patient over what He decreed among them and willed of trials and worldly afflictions regarding them, and they were patient in complying with what He commanded them, and they were patient in abandoning what He forbade them, even if it was contrary to the desires of their souls. So Allah exchanged the hardship and burden they encountered for blessing and ease.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيها عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ لا يَرَوْنَ فِيها شَمْساً وَلا زَمْهَرِيراً** — *reclining therein on couches, they will find therein neither [the heat of] a sun nor [the ordeal of] a biting cold* (76:13). "Couches" (الأرائك) is the plural of *arīkah*, which is that upon which one reclines. "Biting cold" (الزمهرير) is intense cold. The meaning is: while they are reclining in Paradise on couches, they see therein no sun such that they would be pained by its heat, nor biting cold such that they would be pained by its cold.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَدانِيَةً عَلَيْهِمْ ظِلالُها وَذُلِّلَتْ قُطُوفُها تَذْلِيلاً** — *with its shades hovering over them, and its fruits lowered [for them] to reach* (76:14). "Shades" (الظلال) is the plural of *zill*. The "hovering" (دنو) of the shades over them is their proximity to them such that they spread over them; thus, the proximity implies the meaning of spreading. "Fruits" (قطوف) is the plural of *qitf*, which is the plucked, gathered fruit. The "lowering" (تذليل) of the fruits for them is making them subservient to them; they pluck them however they wish without hindrance or burden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِآنِيَةٍ مِنْ فِضَّةٍ وَأَكْوابٍ كانَتْ قَوارِيرَا** — *They will be served with silver vessels and goblets of crystal* (76:15). "Vessels" (الآنية) is the plural of *inā'*, like *aksiyah* is the plural of *kisā'*, which is a container. "Goblets" (أكواب) is the plural of *kūb*, which is a drinking vessel that has neither a handle nor a spout. What is meant is the circling of the "immortal youths" (الولدان المخلدون) around them with the vessels and goblets of drink, as will follow in His saying: "And there will go around them youths..." the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَوارِيرَا مِنْ فِضَّةٍ قَدَّرُوها تَقْدِيراً** — *crystal from silver, which they have measured in a precise measure* (76:16). This is a substitute (بدل) for "crystal" in the previous verse. The crystal being "from silver" is based on eloquent simile (التشبيه البليغ); i.e., they are like silver in purity even if they are not actually from it, as has been said. It is also possible that it is by the omission of a possessed noun (مضاف), the estimation being "from the purity of silver."

The pronoun of the subject in "they have measured" (قَدَّرُوها) refers to the righteous. What is meant by their measuring the vessels and goblets is their being according to what they wish of measure, quenching them such that they neither increase nor decrease, as He, the Exalted, said: **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها** — *They will have therein whatever they wish* (50:35), and He had said before: "making it gush forth as they please."

It is also possible that the pronoun refers to the "circlers" (الطائفين) inferred from His saying: "They will be served." What is meant by their measuring the vessels and goblets is their bringing them according to the measure they desired, containing what they craved in the amount they craved.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُسْقَوْنَ فِيها كَأْساً كانَ مِزاجُها زَنْجَبِيلاً** — *They will be given to drink therein a cup whose mixture is ginger* (76:17). It has been said: They used to find ginger pleasant in drink, so the righteous were promised that, and the ginger of Paradise is more pleasant and delicious.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَيْناً فِيها تُسَمَّى سَلْسَبِيلاً** — *from a spring therein named Salsabil* (76:18). i.e., from a spring, or the estimation is "I mean" or "I specify a spring." Al-Raghib said: "His saying: **سَلْسَبِيلاً** means easy, delicious, smooth, and swift in flow."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ إِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ حَسِبْتَهُمْ لُؤْلُؤاً مَنْثُوراً** — *They will be served by immortal youths. When you see them, you will suppose them to be scattered pearls* (76:19). i.e., youths who are permanent in their state of freshness, splendor, and beauty of appearance. It has been said: i.e., adorned with earrings called *khaladah*.

What is meant by supposing them to be "scattered pearls" is that in the purity of their colors, the radiance of their faces, the reflection of their light upon one another, and their dispersal in their gatherings, they are like scattered pearls.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيماً وَمُلْكاً كَبِيراً** — *As you look on, you will see there bliss and a great kingdom* (76:20). "There" (ثَمَ) is an adverb of place purely for spatiality. Therefore, it has been said: the meaning of the first "you see" (رَأَيْتَ) is "you cast your sight." The meaning is: and when you cast your sight there — meaning Paradise — you will see an indescribable bliss and a great kingdom whose extent cannot be measured.

It has been said: "There" (ثَمَ) is a relative clause whose relative pronoun is omitted, the estimation being: "and when you see what is there of bliss and kingdom." This is like His saying: **لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ** — *The tie between you is now severed* (6:94). The Kufan grammarians allow the omission of the relative pronoun while keeping the relative clause, though the Basrans among them forbid it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عالِيَهُمْ ثِيابُ سُندُسٍ خُضْرٌ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٌ** etc. — *Upon them will be garments of fine green silk and brocade* (76:21). It is apparent that "upon them" (عالِيَهُمْ) is a circumstantial qualifier for the righteous to whom the pronouns refer, and "garments" (ثِيابُ) is its subject. "Fine silk" (السندس) — as has been said — is that which is thinly woven of silk. "Green" (خُضْرٌ) is an attribute of the garments. "Brocade" (الإستبرق) is that which is thickly woven of silk garments; it is an Arabized word like *sundus*.

His saying: **وَحُلُّوا أَساوِرَ مِنْ فِضَّةٍ** — *and they will be adorned with silver bracelets*. "Adorning" (التحلية) is beautification. "Bracelets" (أساور) is the plural of *siwār*, which is well-known. Al-Raghib said: It is Arabized from *dastwārah*.

His saying: **وَسَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَراباً طَهُوراً** — *and their Lord will give them to drink a pure drink*. i.e., reaching the ultimate in purification (التطهير), leaving no impurity but that it removes it. Among impurities is the impurity of heedlessness of Allah, the Glorified, and being veiled from attending to Him. Thus, they are not veiled from their Lord; therefore, it was for them to praise their Lord, as He said: **وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — *and their concluding call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’* (10:10). It has been previously mentioned in the commentary of Surah al-Hamd that "praise" (الحمد) is a description for which none are fit except the sincere (المخلصون) among the servants of Allah, due to His saying: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — *Clear is Allah of what they allege, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants* (37:160).

In His saying: **وَسَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ** — *and their Lord will give them to drink*, He, the Exalted, has dropped all intermediaries and attributed their drinking to Himself. This is the best of what Allah, the Exalted, has mentioned of the bliss granted to them in Paradise. Perhaps it is part of the "increase" (المزيد) mentioned in His saying: **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ** — *They will have therein whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more* (50:35).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ هذا كانَ لَكُمْ جَزاءً وَكانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُوراً** — *‘This is indeed your reward, and your endeavor has been appreciated’* (76:22). This is a narration of what they are addressed with from Him, the Exalted, when He pays them their reward, or by the omission of "saying," the estimation being: "and it is said to them: Indeed this was for you a reward..." etc.

His saying: **وَكانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُوراً** — *and your endeavor has been appreciated* is an initiation of thanks for their pleasing endeavors and accepted deeds. And what a beautiful word it is, by which their souls are comforted!

Know that He, the Exalted, did not mention among the bliss of Paradise mentioned in these verses the women of Paradise from the "fair-eyed houris" (الحور العين), which is among the most important things He mentions when describing the blessings of Paradise in the rest of His speech. It can be inferred from this that among those righteous ones regarding whom the verses were revealed, there was one who was a woman.

It is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: "Among the subtle points (اللطائف), according to the view that the Surah was revealed regarding them — meaning the People of the House (أهل البيت) — is that He, the Glorified, did not mention therein the fair-eyed houris, but explicitly mentioned immortal youths, out of respect for the sanctity of al-Batul [Fatimah] and the delight of the Messenger's eye." End quote.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Itqan* by al-Suyuti, narrated from al-Bayhaqi in *Dala'il al-Nubuwwah* with his chain to Ikrimah and al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan, they said: "Allah sent down of the Quran in Mecca: *Read in the Name of your Lord*, *Nun*, *al-Muzzammil* — until they said — and what was sent down in Medina: *Woe to the defrauders*, *al-Baqarah*, *Al Imran*, *al-Anfal*, *al-Ahzab*, *al-Ma'idah*, *al-Mumtahanah*, *al-Nisa'*, *When the earth is shaken*, *al-Hadid*, *Muhammad*, *al-Ra'd*, *al-Rahman*, and *Has there been for man*." The hadith.

In it, from ibn al-Durays in *Fada'il al-Quran* with his chain to Uthman ibn Ata al-Khurasani from his father from ibn Abbas, he said: "Whenever the opening of a Surah was revealed in Mecca, it was written in Mecca — then Allah would add to it whatever He willed. The first that was revealed of the Quran was *Read in the Name of your Lord*, then *Nun*, then *O you wrapped up* — until he said — then He revealed in Medina Surah *al-Baqarah*, then *al-Anfal*, then *Al Imran*, then *al-Ahzab*, then *al-Mumtahanah*, then *al-Nisa'*, then *When the earth is shaken*, then *al-Hadid*, then *al-Qital* [Muhammad], then *al-Ra'd*, then *al-Rahman*, then *al-Insan*." The hadith.

In it, from al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* with his chain to Mujahid from ibn Abbas that he said: "Indeed, the first that Allah revealed to His Prophet of the Quran was *Read in the Name of your Lord*," and he mentioned the like of the hadith of Ikrimah and al-Hasan — and in it is the mention of three of the Meccan Surahs that were dropped from their two narrations — which are *al-Fatihah*, *al-A'raf*, and *Kaf Ha Ya 'Ayn Sad*. In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, ibn al-Durays, ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from ibn Abbas who said: "Surah *al-Insan* was revealed in Medina."

In it, ibn Mardawayh narrated from ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى حُبِّهِ** — the verse — he said: "This verse was revealed regarding Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)."

**I say:** The verse shares with the rest of the verses of the Surah's opening — whether preceding it or following it — a single, connected context; thus, its revelation regarding the two of them (عليه‌السلام) is inseparable from the revelation of all of them in Medina.

In *al-Kashshaf*: And from ibn Abbas: "That al-Hasan and al-Husayn fell ill, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) visited them with some people who were with him. They said: 'O Abu al-Hasan, if only you would make a vow for your two sons.' So Ali, Fatimah, and Fiddah — a maidservant of theirs — vowed that if they recovered from what afflicted them, they would fast for three days. They recovered, and they had nothing with them.

So Ali borrowed from Sham'un al-Khaybari, the Jew, three measures (*as'u'*) of barley. Fatimah ground one measure and baked five loaves according to their number. They placed them before them to break their fast, when a beggar stood before them and said: 'Peace be upon you, O People of the House of Muhammad! A needy person (مسكين) from the needy of the Muslims; feed me, may Allah feed you from the tables of Paradise.' So they preferred him over themselves and spent the night having tasted nothing but water, and they woke up fasting.

When evening came and they placed the food before them, an orphan (يتيم) stood before them, so they preferred him. And a prisoner (أسير) stood before them on the third day, and they did the same.

When morning came, Ali took the hands of al-Hasan and al-Husayn and they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When he saw them trembling like chicks from the intensity of hunger, he said: 'How much it grieves me to see what I see in you!' So he went with them and saw Fatimah in her prayer niche (محرابها), her back having stuck to her belly and her eyes having sunken. That grieved him, so Gabriel descended and said: 'Take it, O Muhammad; Allah congratulates you regarding your People of the House,' and he recited the Surah to him."

**I say:** The narration is reported through more than one path from Ata from ibn Abbas. Al-Bahrani narrated it in *Ghayat al-Maram* from Abu al-Mu'ayyad al-Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad in the book *Fada'il Amir al-Mu'minin* with his chain to Mujahid from ibn Abbas; and from him with another chain from al-Dahhak from ibn Abbas; and from al-Hamuyini in the book *Fara'id al-Simtayn* with his chain to Mujahid from ibn Abbas; and from al-Tha'labi with his chain to Abu Salih from ibn Abbas. It was also narrated in *al-Majma'* from al-Wahidi in his commentary.

In *al-Majma'*, with his chain to al-Hakim with his chain to Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he said: "I asked the Prophet about the reward of the Quran, so he informed me of the reward of Surah by Surah, according to how they were revealed from heaven. The first that was revealed to him in Mecca was the Opening of the Book, then *Read in the Name of your Lord*, then *Nun*" — until he said — "and the first that was revealed in Medina was Surah *al-Baqarah*, then *al-Anfal*, then *Al Imran*, then *al-Ahzab*, then *al-Mumtahanah*, then *al-Nisa'*, then *When the earth is shaken*, then *al-Hadid*, then Surah *Muhammad*, then *al-Ra'd*, then Surah *al-Rahman*, then *Has there been*." The hadith.

In it, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali in his commentary, he said: "Al-Hasan ibn al-Hasan Abu Abdillah ibn al-Hasan narrated to me: that it is Medinan, the entire Surah was revealed regarding Ali and Fatimah."

In the commentary of al-Qummi, from his father from Abdullah ibn Maymun from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), he said: "Fatimah (عليه‌السلام) had some barley, so they made it into *asīdah* (barley porridge). When they had cooked it and placed it before them, a needy person came and said: 'A needy person, may Allah have mercy on you.' So Ali (عليه‌السلام) stood up and gave him a third. It was not long before an orphan came and said: 'The orphan, may Allah have mercy on you.' So Ali (عليه‌السلام) stood up and gave him a third. Then a prisoner came and said: 'The prisoner, may Allah have mercy on you.' So Ali (عليه‌السلام) gave him a third, and they did not taste it. So Allah, the Glorified, revealed the verses regarding them — and it is applicable to every believer who does that for the sake of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

**I say:** The story, as you see, is summarized in the narration. Al-Bahrani narrated that in *Ghayat al-Maram* from al-Mufid in *al-Ikhtisas* with a chain; and from ibn Babawayh in *al-Amali* with his chain to Mujahid from ibn Abbas; and with his chain to Salamah ibn Khalid from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father (عليه‌السلام); and from Muhammad ibn al-Abbas ibn Mahyar in his commentary with his chain to Abu Kathir al-Zubayri from Abdullah ibn Abbas. In *al-Manaqib*, it is reported from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Ali (عليه‌السلام): in a hadith wherein he says to the people after the death of Umar ibn al-Khattab: "I adjure you by Allah, is there anyone among you regarding whom and his children **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِنْ كَأْسٍ كانَ مِزاجُها كافُوراً** until the end of the Surah was revealed, other than me?" They said: "No."

In the book *al-Khisal*, in the protest of Ali against Abu Bakr, he said: "I adjure you by Allah, am I the subject of the verse — **يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ وَيَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً** — or are you?" He said: "Rather, you."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani, ibn Mardawayh, and ibn Asakir narrated from ibn Umar who said: "A man from Abyssinia came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'Ask and seek understanding.' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you have been preferred over us in colors, forms, and prophethood. Do you see that if I believe in what you believe in and act like what you act like, I shall be with you in Paradise?' He said: 'Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, indeed the whiteness of the black person is seen in Paradise from a distance of a thousand years.' Then he said: 'Whoever says *There is no god but Allah*, he has a covenant with Allah; and whoever says *Glory be to Allah and by His praise*, a hundred thousand good deeds and twenty-four thousand good deeds are written for him.' And the Surah **هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ** until His saying: **مُلْكاً كَبِيراً** was revealed to him.

The Abyssinian said: 'And shall my eye see what your eyes see in Paradise?' He said: 'Yes.' So he fell ill until his soul departed. Umar said: 'I saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) lowering him into his grave with his own hand.'"

In it, Ahmad narrated in *al-Zuhd* from Muhammad ibn Mutarrif, he said: "A trustworthy person narrated to me: that a black man used to ask the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about glorification (*tasbīh*) and declaration of oneness (*tahlīl*). Umar ibn al-Khattab said to him: 'Enough! You have asked too much of the Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'Enough, O Umar!' And **هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ** was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When he reached the mention of Paradise, the black man let out a sigh and his soul departed. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'He died out of longing for Paradise.'"

In it, ibn Wahb narrated from ibn Zayd that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this Surah — **هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ** — and it had been revealed to him while a black man was with him. When he reached the description of the gardens, he let out a sigh and his soul departed. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Longing for Paradise has taken the soul of your companion.'"

**I say:** These three narrations, assuming their authenticity, do not indicate more than the revelation of the Surah being concurrent with the story of the man; as for its being the "occasion of revelation," no. This meaning is more apparent in the last narration. In short, the three narrations do not contradict the revelation of the Surah regarding the People of the House (عليه‌السلام).

Furthermore, ibn Umar's narration of the story, which is apparent in his presence during the event — and he had migrated to Medina when he was eleven years old — is among the evidences of the story's occurrence in Medina.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* also, al-Nahhas narrated from ibn Abbas who said: "Surah *al-Insan* was revealed in Mecca."

**I say:** This is a summary of a long hadith cited by al-Nahhas in the book *al-Nasikh wa al-Mansukh*, and it was quoted in *al-Itqan*. It contradicts what was previously quoted extensively from ibn Abbas regarding the Surah's revelation in Medina and that it was revealed regarding the People of the House (عليه‌السلام).

Moreover, the context of its verses, especially His saying **يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ** and **يُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ** etc., is the context of an actual story. The mention of the prisoner among those they fed is a fine witness to the verses' revelation in Medina, as the Muslims had no prisoners in Mecca, as previously pointed out.

Some have said what is summarized as: "The narrations differ regarding the Meccan or Medinan nature of this Surah. The more likely view is that it is Meccan; rather, it is apparent from its context that it is among the ancient Quranic Surahs revealed in Mecca in the early days of the Mission. This is supported by the detailed and long descriptions of sensory blessings and the descriptions of harsh punishment contained therein. It is also supported by the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient for the judgment of his Lord and not to obey from among them a sinner or an ungrateful one, and to remain firm upon the truth revealed to him and not to compromise with the polytheists — commands that used to be revealed in Mecca when the persecution of the call and its companions intensified, as in Surah *al-Qalam*, *al-Muzzammil*, and *al-Muddaththir*. Thus, no weight is given to the possibility of the Surah being Medinan."

This is invalid. As for what he mentioned regarding the Surah's inclusion of detailed and long descriptions of sensory blessings and descriptions of harsh punishment, this is not something exclusive to Meccan Surahs such that the Surah would be judged Meccan because of it. For Surah *al-Rahman* and Surah *al-Hajj* are Medinan according to what has passed in the narrations containing the order of the revelation of Quranic Surahs, and they both contain detailed and long descriptions of sensory blessings and descriptions of harsh punishment that exceed and surpass this Surah by far.

As for what he mentioned regarding the Surah's inclusion of the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient and not to obey from among them a sinner or an ungrateful one, and not to compromise with them and to remain firm upon the truth revealed to him — the response is that these commands occur in the second section of the Surah's verses, which is His saying: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ تَنْزِيلاً** until the end of the Surah. It is very possible that this section of verses — which possesses a complete, independent context — was revealed in Mecca. This is supported by what is in many of the preceding narrations: that what was revealed regarding the People of the House in Medina is the first section of the verses. Accordingly, the beginning of the Surah is Medinan and its end is Meccan.

Even if we concede its revelation all at once, the command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient is not exclusive to Meccan Surahs. It has appeared in His saying: **وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ وَلا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ وَكانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً** — *And content yourself with those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face, and do not let your eyes pass beyond them, desiring the adornment of the life of the world, and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance, and who follows his own desires, and whose conduct is [mere] profligacy* (18:28). The verse — according to what is narrated — is Medinan, and the verse — as you see — is identical in meaning with His saying: **فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ** etc., and it is in a context very similar to the context of these verses; so refer and reflect.

Furthermore, what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to encounter of injury from the hypocrites, those in whose hearts is a disease, and the coarse ones among those weak in faith, was not easier than the injury from the polytheists in Mecca; the reports of his biography bear witness to that.

There is also no evidence for the restriction of the "sinner" (آثما) and the "ungrateful" (كفورا) to the polytheists of Mecca. There were other disbelievers besides them, and the Quran has affirmed "sin" (الإثم) for a group of Muslims in various instances, such as His saying: **لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ مَا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ** — *Every one of them shall bear what he has earned of sin* (24:11), and His saying: **وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً** — *But whoever commits a sin or an error and then throws the blame for it on an innocent person, he has surely burdened himself with calumny and a manifest sin* (4:112).

In *al-Majma'*, al-Ayyashi narrated with his chain to Abdullah ibn Bukayr from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) about His saying: **لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُوراً**. He said: 'He was a thing, but he was not mentioned.'"

The first to be revealed to him in Mecca was the Opening of the Book (فاتحة الكتاب), then **Read in the Name of your Lord** (اقرأ باسم ربك), then **N** (ن)—until he said—and the first to be revealed in Medina was *Surah al-Baqarah*, then *al-Anfal*, then *Al ‘Imran*, then *al-Ahzab*, then *al-Mumtahanah*, then *an-Nisa’*, then **When the earth is shaken** (إذا زلزلت), then *al-Hadid*, then *Surah Muhammad*, then *ar-Ra‘d*, then *Surah ar-Rahman*, then **Has there [not] come** (هل أتى). [To the end of] the hadith.

In the same source, narrated from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali in his *Tafsir*, he said: Al-Hasan ibn al-Hasan Abu ‘Abdillah ibn al-Hasan narrated to me: "It is Medinan; the entire *Surah* was revealed concerning ‘Ali and Fatimah."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, narrated from his father, from ‘Abdullah ibn Maymun, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Fatimah (peace be upon her) had some barley, so they made it into a porridge (عصيدة) (1). When they had cooked it and placed it before them, a needy person came and said: 'A needy person, may Allah have mercy on you.' So ‘Ali (peace be upon him) stood up and gave him a third. Not long after, an orphan came and said: 'The orphan, may Allah have mercy on you.' So ‘Ali (peace be upon him) stood up and gave him a third. Then a prisoner came and said: 'The prisoner, may Allah have mercy on you.' So ‘Ali (peace be upon him) gave him the [remaining] third. They did not even taste it, so Allah, the Glorified, revealed the verses concerning them—and it remains applicable to every believer who does that for the sake of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

**I say:** The story, as you see, is summarized in the narration. This has been narrated by al-Bahrani in *Ghayat al-Maram* from al-Mufid in *al-Ikhtisas* with its chain of transmission; and from Ibn Babawayh in *al-Amali* via his chain from Mujahid from Ibn ‘Abbas; and via his chain from Salamah ibn Khalid from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him); and from Muhammad ibn al-‘Abbas ibn Mahyar in his *Tafsir* via his chain from Abu Kathir al-Zubayri from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas; and in *al-Manaqib*, it is reported from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah.

__________________
(1) *Al-‘Asidah*: Barley that is moistened with clarified butter and cooked.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, narrated from ‘Ali (peace be upon him): In a tradition where he says to the group after the death of ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab: "I adjure you by Allah, is there anyone among you besides me concerning whom and his children was revealed: **Indeed the pious shall drink from a cup whose mixture is camphor** (إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِنْ كَأْسٍ كانَ مِزاجُها كافُوراً) until the end of the *Surah*?" They said: "No."

In the book *al-Khisal*, regarding the argumentation of ‘Ali against Abu Bakr, he said: "I adjure you by Allah, am I the subject of the verse—**They fulfill their vows and fear a day whose ill is widespread** (يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ وَيَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً)—or are you?" He replied: "Rather, it is you."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: "A man from Abyssinia came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to him: 'Ask and seek understanding.' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you have been preferred over us in color, appearance, and prophethood. Do you see that if I believe in what you believe in and act as you act, I shall be with you in Paradise?' He said: 'Yes, by Him in Whose hand is my soul, the whiteness of the black person shall be seen in Paradise from a distance of a thousand years.' Then he said: 'Whoever says "There is no god but Allah" has a covenant with Allah; and whoever says "Glory be to Allah and by His praise," a hundred thousand and twenty-four thousand good deeds are recorded for him.' Then the *Surah* was revealed to him: **Has there [not] come upon man a period of time** (هَلْ أَتَى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ) until His saying: **and a great kingdom** (مُلْكاً كَبِيراً)."

The Abyssinian said: "And shall my eyes see what your eyes see in Paradise?" He said: "Yes." So he wept until his soul departed. ‘Umar said: "I saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) lowering him into his grave with his own hand."

In the same source, Ahmad narrated in *al-Zuhd* from Muhammad ibn Mutarrif, who said: "A trustworthy person narrated to me that a black man used to ask the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about glorification (التسبيح) and the declaration of oneness (التهليل). ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab said to him: 'Enough! You have asked the Messenger of Allah too much.' He replied: 'Enough, O ‘Umar!' And there was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **Has there [not] come upon man a period of time** (هَلْ أَتَى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ). When he reached the mention of Paradise, the black man let out a sigh and his soul departed. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'He died out of longing for Paradise.'"

In the same source, Ibn Wahb narrated from Ibn Zayd that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this *Surah*—**Has there [not] come upon man a period of time** (هَلْ أَتَى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ)—after it had been revealed to him while a black man was with him. When he reached the description of the gardens, he let out a sigh and his soul departed. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Longing for Paradise has drawn out the soul of your companion."

**I say:** These three narrations, assuming their authenticity, do not indicate more than the revelation of the *Surah* being concurrent with the story of the man; as for it being the cause of revelation (سبب النزول), they do not. This meaning is more apparent in the last narration. In short, these three narrations do not contradict the revelation of the *Surah* concerning the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them).

Furthermore, Ibn ‘Umar’s narration of the story—which suggests his presence during the event, and he had migrated to Medina when he was eleven years old—is among the evidences that the event occurred in Medina.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* as well, al-Nahhas narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "*Surah al-Insan* was revealed in Mecca."

**I say:** This is a summary of a long hadith cited by al-Nahhas in the book *al-Nasikh wa al-Mansukh*, and it was quoted in *al-Itqan*. It contradicts what has been previously narrated through numerous channels (مستفيض) from Ibn ‘Abbas that the *Surah* was revealed in Medina and that it was revealed concerning the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon them).

Moreover, the context of its verses, especially His saying: **They fulfill their vows** (يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ) and **They give food** (يُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ) etc., is the context of an actual occurring story. The mention of the prisoner (الأسير) among those they fed is a fine evidence for the revelation of the verses in Medina, as the Muslims had no prisoners in Mecca, as previously alluded to.

Some have said, in summary: "The narrations differ regarding whether this *Surah* is Meccan or Medinan. The more likely view is that it is Meccan; rather, it is apparent from its context that it is among the ancient *Surahs* of the Quran revealed in Mecca at the beginning of the Mission. This is supported by the detailed and lengthy descriptions of sensory blessings and the descriptions of severe punishment contained therein. It is also supported by the command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient for the command of his Lord, and not to obey any sinner or ungrateful one among them, and to remain firm upon the truth revealed to him, and not to compromise with the polytheists—commands that used to be revealed in Mecca when the persecution against the Call and its followers intensified, as in *Surah al-Qalam*, *al-Muzzammil*, and *al-Muddaththir*. Thus, no weight should be given to the possibility of the *Surah* being Medinan."

This [argument] is invalid. As for his mention that the *Surah* contains detailed and lengthy descriptions of sensory blessings and severe punishment, this is not something exclusive to Meccan *Surahs* such that it could be used to judge the *Surah* as Meccan. For *Surah ar-Rahman* and *Surah al-Hajj* are Medinan according to what has preceded in the narrations containing the order of revelation of the Quranic *Surahs*, yet they contain detailed and lengthy descriptions of sensory blessings and severe punishment that far exceed what is in this *Surah*.

As for his mention that the *Surah* contains the command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient, and not to obey any sinner or ungrateful one among them, nor to compromise with them, and to remain firm upon the truth revealed to him—the response is that these commands occur in the second section of the verses of the *Surah*, which is His saying: **Indeed We have sent down to you the Quran in a gradual revelation** (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ تَنْزِيلاً) until the end of the *Surah*. It is very possible that this section of verses—which possesses a complete and independent context—was revealed in Mecca. This is supported by many of the preceding narrations stating that what was revealed concerning the People of the House in Medina was the first section of the verses. Based on this, the beginning of the *Surah* is Medinan and its end is Meccan.

Even if we were to concede its revelation all at once, the command to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient is not exclusive to Meccan *Surahs*. It has appeared in His saying: **And content yourself with those who pray to their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face; and let not your eyes pass beyond them, desiring the adornment of the life of this world, and do not obey him whose heart We have made heedless of Our remembrance and who follows his desire and whose affair is in excess** (وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ وَلا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ وَكانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً) [Qarai: 18:28]. This verse—according to what is narrated—is Medinan, and as you see, it is identical in meaning to His saying: **So be patient for the decision of your Lord** (فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ) etc., and it is in a context very similar to the context of these verses; so refer back and reflect.

Furthermore, the harm the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) encountered from the hypocrites, those in whose hearts is a disease, and the coarse ones among those weak in faith was no less than the harm from the polytheists in Mecca, as the reports of his biography testify.

There is also no evidence for restricting the "sinner" (آثما) and "ungrateful" (كفورا) to the polytheists of Mecca; there were other disbelievers besides them, and the Quran has affirmed "sin" (الإثم) for a group of Muslims in various instances, such as His saying: **Each of them shall bear the sin that he has earned** (لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ مَا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ) [Qarai: 24:11], and His saying: **And whoever commits a fault or a sin and then blames it on an innocent person, he has certainly burdened himself with calumny and a manifest sin** (وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً) [Qarai: 4:112].

In *al-Majma‘*, al-‘Ayyashi narrated via his chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Bukayr from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) about His saying: **not a thing worth mentioning** (لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُوراً). He said: 'He was a thing, but he was not mentioned.'"

**I say:** It is also narrated therein from ‘Abd al-A‘la, the client of the family of Sam, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): the same.

In the same source, also from al-‘Ayyashi via his chain from Sa‘id al-Hadhdha’ from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "He was mentioned in the knowledge [of Allah] but was not mentioned in the creation."

**I say:** He means that he had a subsistence in the knowledge of Allah, then he was created in actuality and thus became mentioned among those who were created.

In *al-Kafi*, via his chain from Malik al-Juhani from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "He was determined (مقدرا) but not mentioned."

**I say:** This is in the same sense as the previous hadith.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding the verse, he said: "He was not in the knowledge nor in the mention." In another hadith: "He was in the knowledge but was not in the mention."

**I say:** The meaning of the first hadith is that he was not in the knowledge of people nor among those they mention among themselves; the meaning of the second is that he was in the knowledge of Allah but was not mentioned among people.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi* as well, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **a fluid mixture** (أَمْشاجٍ نَبْتَلِيهِ), he said: "The fluid of the man and the woman mixed together."

In *al-Kafi*, via his chain from Humran ibn A‘yan, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful** (إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً). He said: 'Either an acceptor, so he is grateful, or a rejecter, so he is a disbeliever.'"

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated it in his *Tafsir* via his chain from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) similarly. In *al-Tawhid*, via his chain to Hamzah ibn al-Tayyar from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), something close to it, and its wording is: "We made it known to him, whether he be an acceptor or a rejecter."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Jabir ibn ‘Abdillah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — until his tongue expresses for him; then when his tongue expresses for him, he is either grateful or ungrateful—and Allah knows best.'"

In *Amali al-Saduq*, via his chain from al-Sadiq from his father (peace be upon him) in a hadith: **a spring whereat the servants of Allah shall drink, making it gush forth as they please** (عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِها عِبادُ اللهِ يُفَجِّرُونَها تَفْجِيراً). He said: "It is a spring in the house of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that gushes forth to the houses of the prophets and the believers. **They fulfill their vows** (يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ) means ‘Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, al-Husayn (peace be upon them), and their maidservant. **And fear a day whose ill is widespread** (وَيَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً) means [a day that is] frowning and grim. **And they give food, for the love of Him** (وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى حُبِّهِ) means: despite their own desire for the food and their preference for it, **to the needy** (مِسْكِيناً) from the needy of the Muslims, **and the orphan** (وَيَتِيماً) from the orphans of the Muslims, **and the prisoner** (وَأَسِيراً) from the prisoners of the polytheists."

And they say when they feed them: **We feed you only for the sake of Allah. We do not desire any reward from you, nor any thanks** (إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ ـ لا نُرِيدُ مِنْكُمْ جَزاءً وَلا شُكُوراً). He said: "By Allah, they did not say this to them [verbally], but they harbored it within themselves, so Allah informed of what they harbored—they say: 'We do not want a reward (جزاء) by which you compensate us, nor thanks (شكورا) by which you praise us; rather, we only fed you for the sake of Allah and seeking His reward.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Hasan, who said: "The prisoners were polytheists on the day this verse was revealed: **And they give food, for the love of Him, to the needy, the orphan and the prisoner** (وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى حُبِّهِ مِسْكِيناً وَيَتِيماً وَأَسِيراً)."

**I say:** The implication of the narration is the revelation of the verse in Medina. Similar to it is what was narrated therein from ‘Abd ibn Humayd from Qatadah, and what was narrated from Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Jurayh, and what was narrated from ‘Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn ‘Abbas.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas ibn Malik from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: **a day, frowning and fateful** (يَوْماً عَبُوساً قَمْطَرِيراً). He said: "It contracts what is between the eyes."
