In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, via his chain from Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Madani from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the description of Paradise, he said: "And the fruits are near to them—and that is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **Its shades will be close over them, and its clusters will be hanging low** (وَدانِيَةً عَلَيْهِمْ ظِلالُها ـ وَذُلِّلَتْ قُطُوفُها تَذْلِيلاً). Because of their proximity to them, the believer takes from the type of fruit he desires with his mouth while he is reclining. Indeed, the types of fruit say to the friend of Allah: 'O friend of Allah, eat me before you eat this one before me.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying: **immortal youths** (وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ), he said: "Adorned with bracelets (مسورون)."

In *al-Ma‘ani*, via his chain from ‘Abbas ibn Yazid, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) while I was with him one day: 'Inform me about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **And when you look there, you will see bliss and a great kingdom** (وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيماً وَمُلْكاً كَبِيراً). What is this kingdom that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has magnified such that He called it "great"?' He said: 'When Allah admits the people of Paradise into Paradise, He sends a messenger to one of His friends, and he finds the gatekeepers at his door. They say to him: "Wait until we seek permission for you." So the messenger of his Lord does not reach him except by permission; that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **And when you look there, you will see bliss and a great kingdom**.'"

In *al-Majma‘*: **And when you look there, you will see bliss and a great kingdom** (وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيماً وَمُلْكاً كَبِيراً)—"It neither ceases nor perishes"—from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In the same source: **Upon them will be clothes of fine green silk** (عالِيَهُمْ ثِيابُ سُندُسٍ خُضْرٌ). It is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding its meaning: "The clothes will be above them and they shall wear them."

## (A Discourse on Human Identity as Indicated by the Quran)

There is no doubt that in this perceived frame which we call "man," there is a principle of life (مبدأ للحياة) to which consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة) are attributed. The Exalted has expressed this in the discourse on the creation of man—Adam—as the spirit (الروح), and in other places in His speech as the soul (النفس). The Exalted said: **So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration** (فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فقعوا له ساجدين) [Qarai: 15:29, 38:72], and He said: **then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His spirit** (ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ) [Qarai: 32:9].

What first comes to mind from the two verses upon initial consideration is that the spirit and the body are two distinct realities (حقيقتان اثنتان متفارقتان), similar to dough composed of water and flour, and that man is the sum of the two realities. Thus, when the spirit is joined to the body, he is a living human, and when it departs, that is death.

However, this is explained by His saying, the Exalted: **Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’** (قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ) [Qarai: 32:11], as it indicates that the spirit which the taker of souls receives and takes is that which is expressed by the pronoun "you" (كم), which is the human being in his entire reality (بتمام حقيقته), not a part of a whole. Therefore, the meaning of breathing the spirit into the body is making the body itself a human being, not the joining of one thing to another that differs from it in its essence (الذات) and the effects of its essence. Thus, man is a single reality (حقيقة واحدة) during the attachment of his spirit to his body and after the departure of his spirit from the body.

This meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We made him a drop of fluid in a secure lodging. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as yet another creature** (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظاماً فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظامَ لَحْماً ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ) [Qarai: 23:12-14]. So that which Allah produced as "yet another creature" is the very same drop of fluid (النطفة) that became a clinging mass, then a fleshy tissue, then bones.

In the same vein is His saying, the Exalted: **Has there [not] come upon man a period of time when he was not a thing worth mentioning?** (هَلْ أَتَى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُوراً). The qualification of the negated "thing" as being "worth mentioning" implies that he was a thing, but he was not mentioned; he was earth or a drop of fluid, for example, but it was not mentioned that he was "such-and-such a person," then he became that very person.

Thus, the import of His speech, the Exalted, is that man is a single real entity (واحد حقيقي) who is the sole principle for all the natural effects of the body and the spiritual effects, just as he is immaterial (مجرد) in himself from matter (المادة), as indicated by the likes of His saying, the Exalted: **Say, ‘The angel of death... will take your souls’** (قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ), and His saying: **It is Allah who takes the souls at the time of their death** (اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها) [Qarai: 39:42], and His saying: **Then We produced him as yet another creature** (ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ), the explanation of which has already preceded.
